topic: OpenAI Agents SDK v2 Guide 2026: Configurable Memory, Sandbox Orchestration, Filesystem Tools

key_points: Official OpenAI announcement of the April 15, 2026 Agents SDK update
Introduces model-native harness with configurable memory, sandbox-aware orchestration
Codex-like filesystem tools including apply_patch and shell tools
Sandbox support for 7 providers: Blaxel, Cloudflare, Daytona, E2B, Modal, Runloop, Vercel
AGENTS.md for custom instructions, MCP tool use, skills for progressive disclosure
Python-first launch; TypeScript planned for future release
Comprehensive breakdown of the new Agents SDK harness vs. previous pure orchestrator approach
Memory is now intentionally managed — not accidental chat history sprawl
Harness covers memory, tools, orchestration, filesystem access, and context management
Harness abstraction is turnkey but flexible to developers' own stacks
Configurable memory enables long-running agent context persistence
Detailed coverage of apply_patch tool enabling file editing via Editor interface
Shell tool for running commands locally or in hosted containers
Manifest abstraction describes agent workspace including Docker images and env vars
Workspace can mount local files, AWS S3, GCS, Azure Blob, or Cloudflare R2
Native sandbox execution separates compute from orchestration for security
Security-focused analysis of the new sandbox execution model
Agents confined to controlled environments with only required files and code
Native sandbox eliminates need for custom isolation layers
Subagents feature (coming soon) for hierarchical agent orchestration
Code mode enables end-to-end coding workflows within sandboxed environments
AI agent frameworks comparison: LangChain, CrewAI, AutoGen vs OpenAI Agents SDK
OpenAI SDK advantage: built-in tracing, guardrails, and cleanest handoff model
OpenAI SDK limitation: originally model-locked to OpenAI (v2 expands to 100+ LLMs)
LangGraph for production durability; CrewAI for role-based team modeling
AutoGen shifting to maintenance mode as Microsoft focuses on broader Agent Framework

stats.fact: More than 4 million people use OpenAI Codex every week as of 2026, demonstrating massive developer adoption of OpenAI's agentic tools
The April 2026 Agents SDK update shipped on April 15, 2026 — the largest overhaul since the SDK's March 2025 launch replacing the experimental Swarm library
The updated Agents SDK supports over 100 non-OpenAI LLMs via the Chat Completions API, marking a significant shift to model-agnostic design
7 sandbox providers natively supported: Blaxel, Cloudflare, Daytona, E2B, Modal, Runloop, and Vercel — E2B offers $100 free credit for dev/testing
CrewAI scores 82% task success on benchmarks with 1.8s average latency, making it a key competitor in the multi-agent framework space
Storage integration spans AWS S3, Google Cloud Storage, Azure Blob Storage, and Cloudflare R2 for the Agents SDK Manifest workspace abstraction

angles: The shift from 'pure orchestrator' SDK to full execution environment: why OpenAI decided to own the entire harness stack
Memory management done right: how the v2 harness solves the accidental chat history sprawl problem that plagued v1 agents
Sandbox as a security primitive: why running code directly was the biggest risk with autonomous agents and how v2 addresses it
The Codex pipeline brought to developers: apply_patch + shell tool = agents that can actually do software engineering work
Provider-agnostic turn: supporting 100+ LLMs removes the biggest complaint about the original SDK being OpenAI-locked
AGENTS.md as a software contract: how custom instruction files standardize agent behavior across deployments
Enterprise readiness checklist: comparing Agents SDK v2's features against LangGraph, CrewAI, and AutoGen for production workloads
Step-by-step sandbox setup guide for E2B (free tier) vs Modal (production) — practical decision framework for builders
keywords: openai agents sdk E2B Modal Daytona
openai agents sdk long running tasks
